commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=2b023ec3c4352f47591295880c11c09b966c2f15)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a playback timeline helper, switches barge-in truncation to Twilio media timestamps, exports/injects `handleTwilioSocket` for tests, and adds unit plus socket-level truncation tests.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence path: after an assistant item has generated 300ms of audio and the Twilio clock advances well past that, `truncation()` still returns `{ audioEndMs: 300 }`, and the server sends it on the next `speech_started`. I did not run tests because this review must keep the checkout read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The PR needs contributor or maintainer follow-up for a code correction plus live transport proof; automation cannot supply the required Twilio/OpenAI proof for the contributor's setup.

target.title: fix(server): truncate barge-in at the Twilio playback clock, not wall time
target.body:
## Problem

When the caller barges in over the assistant, vox tells the Realtime API how much of the current reply was already spoken via `conversation.item.truncate` with an `audio_end_ms`. Two things make that value wrong:

1. **Wall-clock instead of the playback clock.** `audio_end_ms` was `Date.now() - lastAssistantItemStartedAtMs`, where the start was stamped with `Date.now()` on the first audio delta. The Realtime API streams a whole response in a burst that is far shorter than its real playback duration (several seconds of audio can arrive in a few hundred ms and then sit in Twilio's playout buffer). So wall-clock elapsed since the first chunk has no fixed relationship to how much audio the caller has actually heard.

2. **The item is forgotten on `audio.done`.** `lastAssistantItemStartedAtMs` was set back to `null` on `response.output_audio.done`. Because the audio arrives faster than real time, `done` fires while Twilio is still playing seconds of buffered audio. A barge-in in that window hits the `if (!lastAssistantItemId || !lastAssistantItemStartedAtMs) return;` guard and sends **no truncate at all**, so the model's context believes it finished speaking the whole reply even though the caller cut it off. That desyncs the conversation from that point on.

## Fix

Track Twilio's media-stream presentation clock and use it for truncation, matching the approach in Twilio's own OpenAI Realtime sample.

- Each inbound Twilio `media` frame carries `media.timestamp`, the "Presentation Timestamp in Milliseconds from the start of the stream" ([Twilio docs](https://www.twilio.com/docs/voice/media-streams/websocket-messages)). It advances in real time, so it is the right clock for "how much has played."
- Capture the stream timestamp at the first audio delta of an assistant item as the response start, and on barge-in compute `audio_end_ms = latestMediaTimestamp - responseStart`.
- Keep the item truncatable past `audio.done` (it is cleared only once it has actually been truncated, or when a new item starts), so late barge-ins still truncate correctly.
- Clamp `audio_end_ms` to the audio actually generated for the item. vox does not track Twilio `mark` events, so this bound plays the role the empty mark queue plays in the canonical sample: once the reply has fully played out, the truncation point is the real audio length rather than an ever-growing elapsed value, which keeps it from exceeding the generated audio.

All of this lives in a small, pure `src/playback.ts` (`createPlaybackTimeline`), so the timing logic is unit-testable in isolation. `handleTwilioSocket` delegates to it.

## Tests

- `test/playback.test.ts` covers the timeline directly: it uses the media clock rather than wall time, survives past `audio.done`, clamps to the generated audio length, rolls over on a new item, and stops after `clear()`.
- `test/server-truncate.test.ts` drives the real `handleTwilioSocket` with a fake Twilio socket and a fake Realtime client (injected via a new optional `connect` parameter), and asserts the outgoing `conversation.item.truncate.audio_end_ms` is the played-out offset both during playback and after `audio.done`. Reintroducing the old forget-on-done behavior makes the after-`audio.done` case fail, which is the regression this guards.

Full suite is green (25 tests), `tsgo` typecheck passes, and `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint --deny-warnings` are clean on the changed files.

## Real-run output

Driving the real `handleTwilioSocket` end to end (a fake Twilio socket and a fake Realtime client through the new `connect` seam): a 5-second reply burst, then `response.output_audio.done`, then a caller barge-in at the 1000ms mark of the stream clock. The events the handler actually sends to the Realtime API, in order:

```
session.update, input_audio_buffer.append, input_audio_buffer.append,
input_audio_buffer.append, input_audio_buffer.append, conversation.item.truncate
```

and the truncate it emits:

```
{
  "type": "conversation.item.truncate",
  "item_id": "itemA",
  "content_index": 0,
  "audio_end_ms": 1000
}
```

It truncates at the played-out offset (1000ms into a 5s reply) even though `audio.done` already fired. With the previous forget-on-done behavior restored, the same run sends no truncate at all and the integration test fails on exactly that:

```
✖ barge-in truncates at the media-clock offset even after audio.done
  AssertionError: a truncate must be sent even though audio.done already fired
ℹ pass 1
ℹ fail 1
```

A live end-to-end Twilio + OpenAI Realtime phone call needs a provisioned number and a paid Realtime session, so it is not reproducible here. The run above drives the same production `handleTwilioSocket` through the real Twilio and Realtime message protocol, which is the closest real-behavior proof available in review.

Found and fixed with the help of the Claude CLI.
target.author: devyrpauli

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
